In relation to any hazardous substance or class of substances, means the **generation, processing, treatment, package, storage, transportation, use, collection, destruction, conversion, offering for sale, transfer, or the like** of such hazardous substances. (1 Mark)

**5. "Hazardous Substance" [Section 2(e)]:**
Means any substance or preparation which, by reason of its chemical or physico-chemical properties or handling, is a **risk to health, environment, or property**. (1 Mark)

#### Model Answer (b) — Penal Provisions & Section 13 vs Section 14 (7 Marks)

**1. Section 13 — Penalty for Contravention:**
- Whoever fails to comply with or contravenes any of the provisions of the Act, or the rules made or orders/directions issued under it, shall be punishable with:
  - **Imprisonment for a term which may extend to 5 years** (or 7 years if the failure continues beyond one year after the date of conviction).
  - **OR with fine which may extend to ₹1 lakh**.
  - **OR with both**.
  - Additional fine of up to **₹5,000 per day** for continuing violation after conviction. (2 Marks)

**2. Section 14 — Penalty for Failure to Comply with Orders/Directions:**
- Whoever fails to comply with any **order or direction** issued under the Act shall be punishable with:
  - **Imprisonment for a term which may extend to 2 years** (or 7 years if the failure continues beyond one year).
  - **OR with fine which may extend to ₹1 lakh**.
  - **OR with both**. (1 Mark)

**3. Key Distinction Between Section 13 and Section 14:**

| Basis | Section 13 | Section 14 |
|---|---|---|
| **Trigger** | General contravention of any provision, rule, or order | Failure to comply with **specific orders/directions** |
| **Imprisonment** | Up to **5 years** | Up to **2 years** |
| **Nature** | Wider in scope, applies to overall non-compliance | Narrower, applies only when a specific order has been disobeyed |
| **Strictness** | More stringent | Relatively less stringent |

(1.5 Marks)

**4. Relevant Case Law:**
- In **M.C. Mehta v. Union of India (Oleum Gas Leak Case, 1987)**, the Supreme Court held that an enterprise engaged in a hazardous activity is **absolutely and non-delegably liable** for all damage caused, and such liability is not limited by the provisions of Section 13 or 14 — it is in addition to them.
- The principle of **Polluter Pays** and **Precautionary Principle** was read into EPA, 1986 by the Supreme Court in **Vellore Citizens Welfare Forum v. Union of India (1996)**. (2.5 Marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> 1. **Do not confuse** Section 13 (general contravention, 5 years) with Section 14 (specific order, 2 years) — this is the most common differentiator test.
> 2. **Do not forget** to mention Article 253 as the constitutional basis, not Article 252.
> 3. **Do not skip** the Stockholm Conference, 1972 — without it, EPA, 1986 has no constitutional anchor.
> 4. In long answers, always **link Section 3** of the Act to specific real-world rules (e.g., EIA Notification, Hazardous Waste Rules) — examiners award extra marks for application-based answers.
> 5. The "Cognizance" rule under Section 19 is a high-yield point — remember **no private complaint is maintainable** without the Central Government's consent.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Quick-Recall Bullets for Last-Minute Revision:**

- **Full Name:** Environment (Protection) Act, 1986 (came into force on 19 November 1986).
- **Constitutional Basis:** Article 253 (not Article 252).
- **Triggered by:** Stockholm Conference (1972) + Bhopal Gas Tragedy (1984).
- **Pre-constitutional provisions:** Article 48A (DPSP) and Article 51A(g) (Fundamental Duty) — inserted by 42nd Amendment, 1976.
- **Nature:** Umbrella legislation — covers all pollution.
- **Most Powerful Section:** Section 3 — "all such measures" power of Central Government.
- **Broadest Definition:** Section 2(a) — "environment" — water, air, land + inter-relationships + all living beings + property.
- **Penalty Maximum:** 5 years imprisonment (Section 13) or 2 years (Section 14).
- **Continuing Offence Fine:** Up to ₹5,000/day.
- **Cognizance Rule (Section 19):** No private complaint; only by Central Government / authorized officer.
- **Offences by Companies (Section 16):** Person in charge is deemed guilty.
- **Offences by Government Departments (Section 17):** Head of department deemed guilty.
- **Three Core Principles:** Precautionary Principle, Polluter Pays Principle, Absolute Liability.
- **Key Case Laws:** M.C. Mehta v. Union of India (1987) — Oleum Gas Leak Case; Vellore Citizens Welfare Forum (1996); Indian Council for Enviro-Legal Action v. Union of India (1996).
- **Major Rules Issued Under Section 3:** EIA Notification 2006, Hazardous Waste Rules 2016, Solid Waste Rules 2016, E-Waste Rules 2016, Plastic Waste Rules 2016, MSIHC Rules 1989, CRZ Notification 2019.
- **Practical Significance for Engineers:** Every environmental clearance, EIA, consent to establish/operate, and waste management framework in India ultimately traces its legal authority to **Section 3 of EPA, 1986**.

> **End of Module 3 Notes — EPA, 1986**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: EPA and Rules Made Thereunder
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET418 - ENVIRONMENTAL LAWS AND POLICY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** EPA and Rules Made Thereunder

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Environmental Protection Act (EPA), 1986

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
> The **Environment (Protection) Act, 1986** is an umbrella legislation enacted by the Parliament of India under Article 253 of the Constitution (read with the Stockholm Declaration, 1972) to provide for the protection and improvement of the environment and for matters connected therewith. It was passed in the aftermath of the **Bhopal Gas Tragedy (December 1984)**, which exposed the absence of a comprehensive environmental statute in India.

The Act extends to the **whole of India** including the State of Jammu & Kashmir (now Union Territory) and confers upon the Central Government the sweeping power to take all necessary measures to protect and improve the environment, and to prevent, control, and abate environmental pollution.

### 1.1.1 Key Definitions under Section 2 of the EPA, 1986

| Term | Definition (as per EPA 1986) |
|---|---|
| **Environment** | Includes water, air, and land, and the inter-relationship which exists among and between water, air and land, and human beings, other living creatures, plants, micro-organisms and property. |
| **Environmental Pollutant** | Any solid, liquid or gaseous substance present in such concentration as may be, or tend to be, injurious to environment. |
| **Environmental Pollution** | The presence in the environment of any environmental pollutant. |
| **Hazardous Substance** | Any substance or preparation which, by reason of its chemical or physico-chemical properties or handling, is liable to cause harm to human beings, other living creatures, plants, micro-organisms, property or the environment. |
| **Occupier** | A person who has control over the affairs of a factory or premises, or in relation to any hazardous substance, the person in possession of the substance. |
| **Prescribed** | Prescribed by rules made under the Act. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "EPA as the Umbrella Law":**
> Imagine environmental law in India as a giant umbrella. Before 1986, India had *Water Act (1974)*, *Air Act (1981)*, and *Wildlife Act (1972)*, but no single law governed **"environment"** as a holistic subject. Think of EPA, 1986 as the **central handle of that umbrella** — it doesn't replace the specific laws but provides a *framework* to plug all gaps. Whenever a new pollution problem emerged that wasn't covered (e-waste, biomedical waste, noise, hazardous chemicals), the Central Government simply *unfolds a new spoke* — a new **Rule** — under Section 25 of EPA. This is why EPA is often called the **"Umbrella Act"** of Indian environmental legislation.

### 1.1.2 Salient Features of the EPA, 1986

- **Umbrella Legislation**: Covers all dimensions of environment (air, water, land, noise, hazardous substances, waste).
- **Central Government Dominance**: Vast powers vest in the Central Government, with the State Governments acting as delegated authorities.
- **Rule-Making Power**: Section 25 empowers the Central Government to frame rules on standards, procedures, prohibitions, and restrictions.
- **Penal Consequences**: Section 15 prescribes imprisonment up to **5 years** or fine up to **₹1 lakh**, or both, with additional fine of **₹5,000 per day** for continuing violations.
- **No Civil Liability**: EPA does **not** provide for compensation to victims — it is a *criminal* and *regulatory* law only.
- **Extra-Territorial Jurisdiction**: Applies to ships, aircraft, and any equipment or installations in the Exclusive Economic Zone (EEZ).
- **Constitutional Basis**: Enacted under **Article 253** of the Constitution (resolutions of international conferences — Stockholm, 1972).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Powers of the Central Government (Sections 3–5)

The Central Government is empowered to take **all such measures as it deems necessary or expedient** for the purpose of protecting and improving the quality of the environment and preventing, controlling and abating environmental pollution. Specifically, it may:

- **Section 3(1)**: Plan and execute a nation-wide programme for the prevention, control, and abatement of environmental pollution.
- **Section 3(2)(a)**: Co-ordinate the activities of State Governments, statutory bodies, and other agencies relating to environmental protection.
- **Section 3(2)(b)**: Lay down standards for the quality of the environment — emissions, effluents, ambient air, noise, etc.
- **Section 3(2)(c)**: Prescribe procedures and safeguards for the handling of hazardous substances.
- **Section 3(2)(d)**: Prescribe procedures and safeguards for the prevention of accidents which may cause environmental pollution.
- **Section 3(2)(e)**: Lay down procedures and standards for establishment of industry (environmental clearance-linked).
- **Section 3(2)(f)**: Carry out and sponsor investigations and research relating to environmental pollution.
- **Section 5**: Issue directions in writing to any person, officer, or authority to **close, prohibit, or regulate** any industry, process, or operation — and such directions are **binding**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Section 5 is the strongest enforcement power under EPA** — it allows the Government to shut down non-compliant industries by a simple written order, with **no prior notice** mandated.

## 2.2 Rule-Making Architecture — Section 25 of EPA, 1986

Section 25 is the **engine of the entire EPA framework**. It empowers the Central Government to make rules for all or any of the matters referred to in **Section 3**. All the major environmental rules in India — covering hazardous waste, biomedical waste, e-waste, noise, plastic waste, construction & demolition waste, etc. — are statutory offspring of this single section.

## 2.3 Major Rules Made Under EPA, 1986 (High-Yield Table)

| S.No. | Rule | Year | Subject Matter |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | The Environment (Protection) Rules | **1986** | General framework, emission/effluent standards |
| 2 | The Hazardous Wastes (Management, Handling and Transboundary Movement) Rules | **1989** (revised 2008, 2016) | Hazardous waste management |
| 3 | The Manufacture, Storage and Import of Hazardous Chemical Rules | **1989** | MSIHC Rules for major accident hazard control |
| 4 | The Chemical Accidents (Emergency Planning, Preparedness and Response) Rules | **1996** | On-site and off-site emergency plans |
| 5 | The Bio-Medical Waste (Management and Handling) Rules | **1998** (revised 2016, 2018) | Hospital and clinical waste |
| 6 | The Municipal Solid Waste (Management and Handling) Rules | **2000** (revised 2016) | Solid waste from urban areas |
| 7 | The Noise Pollution (Regulation and Control) Rules | **2000** (amended 2010) | Ambient noise standards |
| 8 | The Batteries (Management and Handling) Rules | **2001** (revised 2022) | Used battery collection |
| 9 | The E-Waste (Management and Handling) Rules | **2011** (revised 2016, 2022) | Electronic waste |
| 10 | The Plastic Waste Management Rules | **2011** (revised 2016) | Plastic ban and recycling |
| 11 | The Construction and Demolition Waste Management Rules | **2016** | C&D debris |
| 12 | The Fly Ash Notification | **1999** (amended) | Utilisation of fly ash from TPPs |
| 13 | The Wetlands (Conservation and Management) Rules | **2010, 2017** | Wetland conservation |
| 14 | The Coastal Regulation Zone (CRZ) Notification | **1991, 2011, 2019** | Coastal zone protection |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Trap**: Students often confuse *Acts* with *Rules*. The 1986 Act is legislation passed by Parliament. The Rules are *subordinate legislation* (delegated legislation) framed by the Central Government under the powers of the Act. All Rules are *legally subordinate* to the Act — the Act is supreme.

## 2.4 Penal Provisions — Section 15 of EPA, 1986

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Penalties under Section 15(1) EPA, 1986:**
> - Imprisonment: **up to 5 years**, OR
> - Fine: **up to ₹1,00,000 (One Lakh)**, OR
> - Both
> - **Additional fine of ₹5,000 per day** for continuing offences (Section 15(2))
> - Offences are **cognizable, bailable, and triable by a Judicial Magistrate** (in some classifications — non-bailable for first 6 months offences per latest amendments)

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA)**: Every industrial project above prescribed thresholds requires EIA clearance under EPA.
- **Consent to Establish (CTE) and Consent to Operate (CTO)** issued by State Pollution Control Boards (SPCBs) derive authority from EPA Sections 25/26 read with the Water Act and Air Act.
- **Hazardous Waste Authorization** for industries under Hazardous Waste Rules, 2016.
- **Biomedical Waste Authorization** for hospitals under BMW Rules, 2016.
- **E-Waste Authorization** for producers and recyclers under E-Waste Rules, 2016.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Framework & Legal Logic

Since this is a legal-policy subject, the "derivations" here represent the **logical deduction chain** showing how Section 25 powers translate into specific Rules, and how a violation is processed through the legal system.

## 3.1 Analytical Chain: From Section 25 to Specific Rules

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Section 25 EPA, 1986} \;&\longrightarrow\; \text{Rule-Making Power} \;&\longrightarrow\; \text{Notification in Official Gazette} \\[4pt]
\text{Notification} \;&\longrightarrow\; \text{Standards \& Procedures} \;&\longrightarrow\; \text{Implementation by SPCB/CPCB} \\[4pt]
\text{Implementation} \;&\longrightarrow\; \text{Authorization / Consent} \;&\longrightarrow\; \text{Compliance Monitoring} \\[4pt]
\text{Non-Compliance} \;&\longrightarrow\; \text{Notice under Section 5} \;&\longrightarrow\; \text{Direction / Closure} \\[4pt]
\text{Continued Violation} \;&\longrightarrow\; \text{Prosecution Section 15} \;&\longrightarrow\; \text{Fine + Imprisonment}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Explanation of Each Transition Step:

**Step 1 — Section 25 to Rule-Making Power:**
Section 25 of the EPA empowers the Central Government to make rules "for all or any of the matters referred to in Section 3." This is the *enabling provision* — the legal seedbed for all subordinate legislation.

**Step 2 — Rule-Making Power to Notification in Official Gazette:**
Once a draft rule is approved by the competent authority (Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change — MoEFCC), it is published in the *Official Gazette of India*. Publication is the constitutional requirement under Section 29 of the Act.

**Step 3 — Notification to Standards and Procedures:**
Each rule specifies technical standards (e.g., PM10 ≤ 60 μg/m³, noise ≤ 55 dB(A) for residential zones) and procedural requirements (e.g., how to obtain authorization).

**Step 4 — Implementation by SPCB/CPCB:**
The Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) co-ordinates at the national level, while State Pollution Control Boards (SPCBs) implement the rules at the state level.

**Step 5 — Authorization/Consent:**
Industries must obtain "Consent to Establish" (before construction) and "Consent to Operate" (before production). For hazardous wastes, a separate "Authorization" is required.

**Step 6 — Compliance Monitoring:**
SPCBs conduct inspections, monitor stack emissions, effluent discharge quality, ambient air, etc. CPCB sets national standards; SPCBs enforce them.

**Step 7 — Non-Compliance to Notice under Section 5:**
If violations are found, SPCB/CPCB issues a written direction under Section 5 of the Act.

**Step 8 — Continued Violation to Prosecution:**
If the violator does not comply, prosecution is launched under Section 15, leading to judicial proceedings.

**Step 9 — Prosecution to Fine/Imprisonment:**
The court, upon conviction, imposes penalties as specified in Section 15.

## 3.2 Critical Analysis: Why EPA, 1986 has Survived 40+ Years

| Strategic Strength | Real-World Impact |
|---|---|
| **Flexibility via Section 25** | Allowed quick response to new pollution challenges (e-waste, biomedical, C&D, plastic) without fresh Acts of Parliament |
| **Centralized Authority** | Avoided jurisdictional conflicts in inter-state pollution (e.g., river disputes) |
| **Cross-Media Coverage** | Water + Air + Land + Noise addressed under single statute |
| **Deterrent Penalties** | Section 15 created a credible threat — ₹1 lakh + 5 years imprisonment in 1986 was a serious deterrent |
| **Constitutional Foundation** | Article 253 backing made it immune to most legal challenges |

## 3.3 Limitations and Judicial Critiques

- **No Civil Compensation**: Victims cannot claim damages under EPA — they must go to the *National Green Tribunal (NGT)* under NGT Act, 2010, or civil courts under tort law.
- **Low Fine vs. Profit Motive**: Critics argue ₹1 lakh is trivial for large industries. The Supreme Court has, in several cases, imposed higher *environment compensation* based on the **"Polluter Pays"** principle (Indian Council for Enviro-Legal Action v. Union of India, 1996).
- **Bureaucratic Delays**: Rule-making is often slow; amendments (e.g., E-Waste Rules) have lagged technological evolution.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: EPA, 1986 — Umbrella Structure

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    C["Constitution of India Article 253"] --> A["Environment Protection Act 1986"]
    A --> S25["Section 25 Rule-Making Power"]
    A --> S3["Section 3 Preventive Measures"]
    A --> S5["Section 5 Directions Power"]
    A --> S15["Section 15 Penalties"]
    S25 --> R1["Environment Protection Rules 1986"]
    S25 --> R2["Hazardous Waste Rules 2008 / 2016"]
    S25 --> R3["Biomedical Waste Rules 2016"]
    S25 --> R4["E Waste Rules 2016 / 2022"]
    S25 --> R5["Municipal Solid Waste Rules 2016"]
    S25 --> R6["Plastic Waste Rules 2016"]
    S25 --> R7["Construction Demolition Rules 2016"]
    S25 --> R8["Noise Pollution Rules 2000"]
    S3 --> B1["CPCB National Standards"]
    S3 --> B2["SPCB Implementation"]
    S5 --> E1["Inspection and Closure Orders"]
    S15 --> P1["Imprisonment up to 5 yrs"]
    S15 --> P2["Fine up to 1 Lakh"]
    S15 --> P3["Additional 5000 per day"]
    B2 --> R1
    B2 --> R2
    B2 --> R3
    B2 --> R4
    B2 --> R5
    B2 --> R6
    B2 --> R7
    B2 --> R8
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Rule-Making Flow (Section 25 Mechanism)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Identification of Pollution Gap"] --> B["Drafting by MoEFCC"]
    B --> C["Inter-Ministerial Consultation"]
    C --> D["Cabinet Approval"]
    D --> E["Notification in Official Gazette"]
    E --> F["Standards / Procedures Published"]
    F --> G["Implementation by SPCB / CPCB"]
    G --> H["Authorization to Industries"]
    H --> I{"Compliance?"}
    I -->|Yes| J["Continued Operation"]
    I -->|No| K["Section 5 Direction"]
    K --> L{"Complied?"}
    L -->|No| M["Section 15 Prosecution"]
    M --> N["Court: Fine + Imprisonment"]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Hierarchy of Environmental Governance Under EPA

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    T["Supreme Court and National Green Tribunal"] --> U["Union Government MoEFCC"]
    U --> N["Central Pollution Control Board CPCB"]
    N --> S["State Pollution Control Boards SPCBs"]
    S --> D["District Level Authorities"]
    S --> I1["Industries Hospitals ULBs"]
    S --> I2["Hazardous Waste Generators"]
    S --> I3["Biomedical Waste Handlers"]
    S --> I4["E Waste Producers and Recyclers"]
    S --> I5["Municipal Bodies"]
    S --> I6["Plastic Waste Processors"]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Penalty Computation under Section 15

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    V["Violation Detected"] --> D["Direction under Section 5"]
    D --> Q{"Period of Default in Days"}
    Q -->|D days| F1["Base Fine up to 1 Lakh"]
    Q -->|D days| F2["Daily Fine 5000 x D days"]
    F1 --> T["Total Penalty"]
    F2 --> T
    V --> P["Imprisonment up to 5 Years"]
    T --> C["Conviction by Magistrate"]
    P --> C
    C --> R["Compounding Allowed in Some Cases"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**"What is an Umbrella Act? Explain how the Environment (Protection) Act, 1986 functions as an umbrella legislation in India."**

**Model Answer:**

An *Umbrella Act* is a comprehensive statute that covers a wide range of related subjects under a single legal framework, providing an overarching authority which can be supplemented by subordinate legislation for specific issues.

The **Environment (Protection) Act, 1986** functions as an umbrella legislation because:
1. It covers all three media — **air, water, and land** — under one statute.
2. It empowers the Central Government to frame **rules** for matters not specifically covered (Section 25).
3. All major environmental rules in India — Hazardous Waste Rules, Biomedical Waste Rules, E-Waste Rules, Plastic Waste Rules, Municipal Solid Waste Rules, Noise Pollution Rules — are *notified under Section 25 of EPA*.
4. It serves as a *gap-filling* statute, complementing the Water Act, 1974 and the Air Act, 1981.

Hence, EPA is called the *Umbrella Act* of Indian environmental law.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**"List any six major rules notified under the Environment (Protection) Act, 1986."**

**Model Answer:**

The following major rules have been notified under Section 25 of the EPA, 1986:

1. The Environment (Protection) Rules, **1986**
2. The Hazardous Wastes (Management, Handling and Transboundary Movement) Rules, **2008** (revised **2016**)
3. The Bio-Medical Waste Management Rules, **2016**
4. The E-Waste Management Rules, **2016** (amended **2022**)
5. The Municipal Solid Waste Management Rules, **2016**
6. The Plastic Waste Management Rules, **2016**
7. The Construction and Demolition Waste Management Rules, **2016**
8. The Noise Pollution (Regulation and Control) Rules, **2000** (amended **2010**)

(Any *six* to be enumerated with subject matter.)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Choice Question (1 × 14 = 14 Marks)

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the salient features of the Environment (Protection) Act, 1986. Discuss the powers of the Central Government under Sections 3 and 5 of the Act. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss in detail the salient provisions of the Hazardous and Other Wastes (Management and Transboundary Movement) Rules, 2016. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Answer for Question A (a) — 7 Marks

The salient features of the Environment (Protection) Act, 1986 are:

**1. Umbrella Character:**
EPA, 1986 is an umbrella legislation covering all aspects of environment — air, water, land, noise, hazardous substances — and provides a unified legal framework for environmental protection in India.

**2. Constitutional Basis:**
The Act was enacted under **Article 253** of the Constitution to give effect to the decisions taken at the **United Nations Conference on the Human Environment held at Stockholm in 1972**.

**3. Centralised Authority:**
The Act vests vast powers in the **Central Government** which can:
- Plan and execute nationwide environmental programmes (Section 3(1)).
- Co-ordinate activities of State Governments and statutory bodies (Section 3(2)(a)).
- Lay down standards for environmental quality (Section 3(2)(b)).
- Prescribe procedures and safeguards for hazardous substance handling (Section 3(2)(c)).
- Prescribe procedures for prevention of industrial accidents (Section 3(2)(d)).
- Lay down standards for industrial establishment (Section 3(2)(e)).

**4. Powers under Section 5:**
The Central Government may issue **directions in writing** to any person, officer, or authority, including the power to:
- Close any industry, process, or operation.
- Prohibit or regulate the supply of electricity, water, or any other service.
- These directions are **binding and non-compliance is punishable under Section 15**.

**5. Rule-Making Power (Section 25):**
The Central Government is empowered to make rules for all matters referred to in Section 3. This is the basis of all environmental rules in India.

**6. Penal Provisions (Section 15):**
- Imprisonment up to **5 years**, or
- Fine up to **₹1 lakh**, or both.
- Additional fine of **₹5,000 per day** for continuing violations.

**7. Extra-Territorial Application:**
Extends to the whole of India and applies to ships, aircraft, and installations in the **Exclusive Economic Zone (EEZ)**.

**8. Cognizance of Offence:**
Offence is **cognizable** and triable by a Judicial Magistrate of First Class (per the criminal procedure classification).

---

#### Model Answer for Question A (b) — 7 Marks

The **Hazardous and Other Wastes (Management and Transboundary Movement) Rules, 2016** superseded the earlier 2008 Rules. Salient provisions:

**1. Applicability (Rule 3):**
Applies to the management of hazardous and other wastes as specified in **Schedules I, II, and III** of the Rules.

**2. Duties of the Occupier (Rule 4):**
The occupier generating hazardous waste must:
- Take all practical steps to ensure the waste is **handled, stored, transported, treated, and disposed of safely**.
- Maintain records of hazardous waste generated, stored, treated, and disposed of.
- Submit an **annual return** to the State Pollution Control Board (SPCB).

**3. Authorization (Rule 6):**
No occupier shall handle hazardous waste without obtaining **authorization** from the SPCB. Authorization is granted for a fixed period and is renewable.

**4. Treatment, Storage, and Disposal Facility (TSDF) (Rule 8):**
Hazardous waste must be sent only to a **registered TSDF**. Each TSDF must be designed per CPCB guidelines with liner, leachate collection, and monitoring systems.

**5. Packaging, Labelling, and Transport (Rule 9):**
- Waste must be **packed in sound containers** with clear labels.
- Transport must be accompanied by **Form 10** (consignor declaration) and **Form 11** (transporter declaration).

**6. Import and Export (Rules 11–14):**
- **Basel Convention** principles apply — hazardous waste cannot be imported for final disposal in India.
- Export requires prior informed consent of the destination country.
- **Schedule VI** lists wastes prohibited from import/export.

**7. Liability of Occupier (Rule 21):**
- The occupier is **liable for damages to the environment or third parties** caused by improper handling.
- The occupier must pay **environment compensation** as per CPCB guidelines.

**8. Annual Inventory (Rule 18):**
Each occupier handling more than the threshold quantity must submit an **Annual Inventory** of hazardous waste to the SPCB.

**9. Penalty:**
Violation is punishable under **Section 15 of EPA, 1986** — imprisonment up to 5 years and/or fine up to ₹1 lakh.

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** "The Environment (Protection) Act, 1986 is a post-Bhopal legislation." Explain the background and objectives of the Act. Discuss in detail the penal provisions of the Act. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the salient features of the E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2016. Mention the role of Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) in e-waste management. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Answer for Question B (a) — 7 Marks

**1. Background — The Bhopal Gas Tragedy:**
On the night of **2–3 December 1984**, the Union Carbide India Limited (UCIL) plant in Bhopal released **40 tons of Methyl Isocyanate (MIC)** gas, killing over **3,800 people** immediately and affecting more than 500,000. Investigations revealed:
- Absence of a comprehensive environmental statute.
- Lack of safety standards for hazardous chemical storage.
- No statutory mechanism to hold multinational corporations accountable.

**2. Need for Comprehensive Legislation:**
The Bhopal disaster highlighted the urgent need for a central law that could:
- Regulate hazardous substances, industries, and processes.
- Provide deterrent penalties.
- Empower the Central Government to set standards.

**3. Constitutional Basis:**
The Act was enacted under **Article 253** of the Constitution to implement the **Stockholm Declaration (1972)** and the **Rio Declaration (1992)**.

**4. Objectives of the EPA, 1986:**
- To protect and improve the environment.
- To prevent, control, and abate environmental pollution.
- To establish authorities for environmental protection.
- To lay down standards and prescribe procedures for industrial operations.
- To ensure harmonious development without ecological degradation.

**5. Penal Provisions (Section 15):**

| Type of Penalty | Quantum |
|---|---|
| Imprisonment | Up to **5 years** |
| Fine | Up to **₹1,00,000** |
| Continuing violation | Additional **₹5,000 per day** after conviction |
| Offence classification | Cognizable, bailable (per most judicial interpretations) |
| Court | Judicial Magistrate of the First Class |

**6. Offences by Companies (Section 16):**
Where an offence is committed by a company:
- The person in charge of business (Director, Manager, Secretary) is deemed guilty.
- The company itself is also liable.
- However, if the offence occurred **without the knowledge** of such person, or he **exercised all due diligence** to prevent it, he is not liable.

**7. Compounding of Offences (Section 14):**
Offences under the Act may be compounded by the prescribed authority, subject to conditions.

---

#### Model Answer for Question B (b) — 7 Marks

**1. Background:**
India is the **third-largest e-waste generator** globally. The E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2016 superseded the 2011 Rules and were later amended in **2022** to strengthen Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) and bring in Producer Responsibility Organizations (PROs).

**2. Applicability (Rule 2):**
Applies to every **producer, manufacturer, consumer, bulk consumer, collection centre, dismantler, and recycler** of electrical and electronic equipment (EEE) listed in **Schedule I**.

**3. Categories of EEE (Schedule I):**
- Information Technology equipment
- Consumer electronics
- Telecommunication equipment
- Lighting equipment
- Electrical and electronic tools (with the exception of large-scale stationary industrial tools)
- Toys, leisure, and sports equipment
- Medical devices
- Monitoring and control instruments
- Automatic dispensers

**4. Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) — Rule 4:**
EPR is the **cornerstone** of the 2016 Rules. Key features:
- **Producers** are responsible for the **end-of-life management** of their products.
- They must set up collection mechanisms, take-back systems, or partner with PROs.
- The EPR target is based on **weight recovery percentage** and must increase progressively (e.g., 70% in Year 1, 80% in Year 2, etc., depending on the equipment category — revised 2022 targets).
- Producers must purchase **EPR certificates** from registered recyclers/dismantlers to fulfil their obligations.

**5. Collection Target:**
Producers must achieve a phased collection target of **used EEE generated** as a percentage of the quantity of EEE placed in the market in the previous year.

**6. Authorization (Rule 12):**
- Dismantlers and recyclers must obtain **one-time authorization** from the SPCB.
- Producers must register on the **EPR Portal** of the Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB).

**7. Reduction of Hazardous Substances (RoHS — Rule 13):**
EEE must not contain:
- Lead (Pb) > 0.1%
- Mercury (Hg) > 0.1%
- Cadmium (Cd) > 0.01%
- Hexavalent Chromium (Cr⁶⁺) > 0.1%
- PBBs and PBDEs > 0.1%

Exemptions are given for specific applications listed in **Schedule II**.

**8. Role of EPR — Why It Matters:**
- **Internalises externalities** — the cost of disposal is paid by the producer, not the public.
- **Drives eco-design** — producers are motivated to design easily recyclable products.
- **Funds formal recycling** — EPR revenue supports registered recyclers and dismantlers.
- **Reduces informal sector dominance** — formalizes the recycling chain.
- **Polluter Pays Principle** — directly operationalised in the electronics sector.

**9. Penalties:**
Violations are punishable under **Section 15 of EPA, 1986**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Conflating Rules with Acts** — Many students answer "E-Waste Act, 2016" — wrong! It is a *Rule* under EPA, 1986.
> 2. **Forgetting Section 25 as the Parent Provision** — Always cite Section 25 of EPA, 1986 as the legal parent of every environmental Rule.
> 3. **Missing the 2016 / 2022 Amendment** — For E-Waste and BMW, mention the most recent amendment year.
> 4. **Not Linking Polluter Pays Principle** — Examiner awards bonus marks when you connect rules to the Polluter Pays Principle (Supreme Court, Vellore Citizens Welfare Forum, 1996) and the Precautionary Principle.
> 5. **Skipping the Article 253 background** — Examiners expect a 2-line mention of the Bhopal tragedy and Article 253.
> 6. **Vague Section numbers** — Always cite specific section numbers: Section 3(2)(b), Section 5, Section 15, Section 25, Section 16.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **EPA, 1986** = **Umbrella Act** — covers all environmental media.
- **Constitutional basis** = **Article 253** (Stockholm Declaration, 1972).
- **Triggered by** = **Bhopal Gas Tragedy, December 1984** (Union Carbide, MIC leak).
- **Section 3** = Preventive measures & standards.
- **Section 5** = Direction-issuing power (close, prohibit, regulate).
- **Section 15** = Penalty (5 yrs jail OR ₹1 lakh fine OR both; ₹5,000/day extra).
- **Section 16** = Liability of companies and officers.
- **Section 25** = Rule-making power — the **parent of all Rules**.
- **Key Rules (year)** = HW (2016), BMW (2016), E-Waste (2016, amended 2022), MSW (2016), Plastic (2016), C&D (2016), Noise (2000, amended 2010).
- **Polluter Pays Principle** = Supreme Court, *Indian Council for Enviro-Legal Action v. UoI (1996)* & *Vellore Citizens Welfare Forum (1996)*.
- **Precautionary Principle** = When harm is suspected, action must precede certainty.
- **Sustainable Development Principle** = Balance between development and ecology (Brundtland, 1987).
- **CPCB** = apex technical body for standards.
- **SPCB** = state-level implementation authority.
- **MoEFCC** = nodal Ministry for framing Rules.
- **NGT** (2010) = adjudicating forum for civil compensation, not part of EPA but linked.
- **Public Trust Doctrine** = State is a trustee of natural resources.
- **Absolute Liability** = *M.C. Mehta v. Union of India (Oleum Gas Leak case, 1987)* — applicable to hazardous industries under EPA.
- **Examination Tip** = Always begin EPA-related answers with the Bhopal tragedy and Article 253.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: PLI Act, 199; Principles of Strict and Absolute Liability
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET418 - ENVIRONMENTAL LAWS AND POLICY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** PLI Act, 199; Principles of Strict and Absolute Liability

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definitions & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Public Liability Insurance Act, 1991 (PLI Act)

### Formal Definition
The **Public Liability Insurance Act, 1991** is an Indian environmental legislation enacted to provide for mandatory **public liability insurance** for the purpose of providing immediate relief to the persons affected by accidents occurring while handling any hazardous substance. The Act is also known as the **"Public Liability Insurance Act, 1991"** and came into force with effect from **1st April 1991**. It is administered by the Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change (MoEFCC), Government of India.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definition (KTU High-Yield):** The PLI Act, 1991 is a "no-fault" liability legislation — the claimant need not prove negligence, fault, or wrongful act of the owner. Liability is **vicarious, statutory, and immediate**.

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine a chemical factory located in your neighborhood. One day, a toxic gas leaks and 200 residents fall sick. Under normal tort law, each of the 200 victims would have to fight separate, expensive, decade-long court cases to prove the factory was negligent. The PLI Act **skips this entire legal drama** — the moment an accident occurs, the insurance company of the factory *must* pay relief money to victims within a strict timeframe. It is like a **mandatory "first-aid" insurance policy for industrial disasters** — the victim gets treated first, while the question of "who is really at fault" is settled later (or not at all).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why PLI Act, 1991?** Before this Act, victims of the **Bhopal Gas Tragedy (1984)** were left without immediate relief for years. The Act was a direct legislative response to such industrial disasters, ensuring **speedy, no-fault, minimum relief**.

### Key Constants & Statutory Metrics (Bolded)
- **Threshold Quantity** of hazardous substance: as specified in the **Schedule I** of the Rules (the moment you cross this quantity, the Act applies).
- **Maximum Relief for Fatal Accidents**: **Rs. 5,00,000 per person** (subsequently revised by Government notifications).
- **Relief for Permanent Total Disability**: Equal to fatal accident relief.
- **Relief for Partial Disability / Injury**: Proportionate amount based on nature of injury.
- **Minimum relief for hospitalization**: Based on number of days hospitalized.
- **Statutory Time Limit for Claim**: Relief must be paid within **3 months** from the date of application.

---

## 1.2 Principles of Strict Liability (Rule in *Rylands v Fletcher*)

### Formal Definition
The rule of **Strict Liability**, as laid down in the landmark English case ***Rylands v Fletcher* (1868)** and later adopted by the Indian Supreme Court, states that a person who for his own purposes brings onto his land and collects and keeps there anything likely to cause mischief if it escapes, is strictly liable for all damage which is the natural consequence of its escape. There is **no need to prove negligence** — liability arises the moment an escape occurs and damage results.

### Conceptual Analogy
Think of **Strict Liability as owning a wild, untamed tiger as a pet**. You keep it safely locked in a strong cage on your property. If one day the tiger escapes and bites a passerby, you are liable for damages — even if you did everything humanly possible to keep the cage locked. The law says: "You chose to keep a dangerous thing; therefore you bear the risk." The **escape** is the trigger, not the negligence.

> [!NOTE]
> **Strict Liability ≠ Absolute Liability.** Strict liability still allows certain defences (Act of God, Act of Third Party, Statutory Authority, Plaintiff's own default, etc.). The owner can *escape* liability if he proves one of these exceptions.

---

## 1.3 Principles of Absolute Liability (Rule in *MC Mehta v Union of India*)

### Formal Definition
The rule of **Absolute Liability**, evolved by the Supreme Court of India in ***MC Mehta v Union of India* (Oleum Gas Leak Case, 1987)**, holds that an enterprise engaged in a hazardous or inherently dangerous activity is **absolutely and unconditionally liable** for any harm resulting from such activity, and no defence (including those available under *Rylands v Fletcher*) is available to such enterprise. The enterprise is **strictly and absolutely liable to compensate** for all damage, regardless of precautions taken or care exercised.

### Conceptual Analogy
Continue with the tiger analogy: **Absolute Liability** is owning the tiger and then **operating a public zoo that charges entry fees**. Here, you are not a private citizen — you are running a **commercial, hazardous enterprise** for profit. The Supreme Court says: "Since you profit from the danger, you bear the full and final cost of any damage — no excuses, no defences, no escape clauses." It is a **non-negotiable cost of doing dangerous business**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Absolute Liability in India?** The Indian Supreme Court found *Rylands v Fletcher* outdated and unsuitable for modern industrial hazards (especially after Bhopal, 1984). In *MC Mehta*, Justice P.N. Bhagwati evolved a **new, stricter, Indian doctrine** that removed all the "escape routes" (defences) available under the old English rule.

### The Key Metric: The "Polluter Pays" Principle
The Court held that liability is co-extensive with **the enterprise's capacity to pay**, and the measure of damages must be **proportional to the magnitude of the enterprise and the harm caused**, not limited by artificial caps.

| Doctrine | Country of Origin | Key Case | Defences Available? |
|---|---|---|---|
| Strict Liability | England (1868) | *Rylands v Fletcher* | Yes (limited defences) |
| Absolute Liability | India (1987) | *MC Mehta v UoI* | No (zero defences) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Salient Features of the PLI Act, 1991 — Step-by-Step Logic

### Operational Logic (How the Act Works)
1. **Identification of Owner**: The Act applies to every **"owner"** handling a **"hazardous substance"** equal to or exceeding the **threshold quantity** specified in **Schedule I** of the Public Liability Insurance Rules, 1991.
2. **Mandatory Insurance (Section 4)**: The owner MUST take out an **Insurance Policy** before handling the hazardous substance, covering liability for death/injury to third parties arising from an accident.
3. **Limit of Liability**: The policy must cover liability up to the **"paid-up capital"** of the undertaking, or a maximum amount as notified by the Central Government, whichever is less.
4. **No-Fault Claim (Section 5)**: Any person who has suffered injury, disability, or death can claim relief by filing a **Claim Application** with the Collector of the District where the accident occurred.
5. **Relief by Collector**: The **District Collector** is the designated authority to adjudicate claims and award relief. No civil court has jurisdiction.
6. **Establishment of Environmental Relief Fund (Section 7A)**: For situations where the owner fails to pay, where the insurance is inadequate, or where claims are made against the owner for damages exceeding the insurance liability.
7. **Establishment of Claim Commissioner / Appellate Authority**: A hierarchy of appeal is provided — **Claims Commissioner → National Environment Tribunal → Supreme Court**.

### Why Each Step Matters
- The Act is **precautionary in nature** — it forces hazardous industries to internalize the cost of potential accidents (via insurance premiums).
- It is **compensatory in nature** — it ensures victims get speedy relief without protracted litigation.
- It is **regulatory in nature** — the Collector acts as a quasi-judicial authority to keep the process accessible.

> [!NOTE]
> **Important Statutory Reference:** The PLI Act has only **26 Sections** and a short Schedule, making it one of the most concise but powerful environmental statutes in India.

---

## 2.2 The Rule of Strict Liability — Six Mandatory Conditions (Per *Rylands v Fletcher*)

For the rule to apply, the following conditions must be satisfied cumulatively:

1. **Bringing & Accumulation**: The defendant has brought and accumulated (collected) a thing on his land.
2. **For His Own Purposes**: It was brought for the defendant's own use or benefit.
3. **Non-Natural Use of Land**: The use of land must be **"non-natural"** — i.e., an extraordinary, special, or unusual use, beyond the ordinary use of land in the locality.
4. **Likely to Cause Mischief if Escapes**: The thing accumulated must be of a dangerous nature — likely to cause harm if it escapes.
5. **Escape**: The dangerous thing must **escape** from the defendant's land to a place outside his occupation/control.
6. **Damage**: The escape must cause damage to the plaintiff.

> [!NOTE]
> **"Non-Natural Use" — The Critical Trigger:** In *Rylands*, the defendant had built a reservoir on his land. The court held that storing **large quantities of water on a hill** was a non-natural use, even though water itself is harmless.

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Strict Liability)

| Element | Requirement | Case Law Illustration |
|---|---|---|
| Bringing + Accumulation | Defendant brought the substance | *Rylands v Fletcher* — water stored in reservoir |
| Non-Natural Use | Use must be unusual/special | *Chasemore v Richards* — water for mill |
| Dangerous Substance | Likely to cause mischief if escapes | *Bhopal Gas Tragedy* — MIC gas |
| Escape | Must escape from defendant's control | *Giles v Walker* — thistle did not escape |
| Damage | Must cause actual harm | *Cambridge Water Co. v Eastern Counties Leather* |
| Defence (6) | See exceptions below | *Read v Lyons* — strict liability not for ultra-hazardous activities inside plant |

---

## 2.3 The Six Exceptions (Defences) to Strict Liability

The defendant is **not liable** if he proves any of the following:

1. **Act of God (Vis Major)**: An unforeseeable, irresistible natural event (e.g., earthquake, cyclone, unprecedented flood) — the defendant could not have reasonably anticipated or prevented.
2. **Act of Third Party / Stranger**: An independent, unauthorized act of a third party over which the defendant had no control, **not attributable to the defendant's negligence**.
3. **Plaintiff's Own Default**: The damage was caused due to the plaintiff's own contributory negligence or deliberate act.
4. **Statutory Authority**: The accumulation was made under compulsion of law (e.g., a municipal waterworks ordered to store water).
5. **Consent of the Plaintiff**: The plaintiff voluntarily and knowingly agreed to bear the risk.
6. **Common Benefit**: The accumulation was for the **common benefit** of the plaintiff and the defendant (e.g., a shared irrigation tank).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Hot Question Favourite:** *"State and explain the exceptions to the rule in Rylands v Fletcher."* — Appears in almost every ESE paper. Memorize all six.

---

## 2.4 The Rule of Absolute Liability — Per *MC Mehta v Union of India* (1987)

### The Five Mandatory Conditions (Per Bhagwati J.)
1. **Enterprise (Industrial / Commercial Activity)**: There must be an enterprise engaged in an activity.
2. **Hazardous or Inherently Dangerous Activity**: The activity must be such that it involves a **real and substantial risk** of harm to persons living in the surrounding area.
3. **Harm Resulted**: The harm must have actually occurred (death, injury, damage to property, or environmental degradation).
4. **Causation**: The harm must be a direct consequence of the activity.
5. **Defence = NIL**: No defence is available, irrespective of care taken or precautions exercised.

### Two Mandatory Obligations on the Enterprise
1. **Absolute Liability to Compensate**: The enterprise is absolutely liable to pay compensation to victims.
2. **No Plea of Inadequate Insurance**: The enterprise cannot argue that its insurance is too small — it must pay from its pocket for the shortfall.

### The "Polluter Pays" Principle (Codified)
The measure of compensation must be:
- **Proportionate to the harm caused**, AND
- **Commensurate with the capacity of the enterprise to pay**.

This ensures that **giant, profitable, hazardous industries** pay huge damages, while a small workshop pays less — a **graduated liability** system.

---

## 2.5 KTU Comparison Master Table: Strict vs Absolute Liability

| Parameter | Strict Liability (*Rylands v Fletcher*, 1868) | Absolute Liability (*MC Mehta v UoI*, 1987) |
|---|---|---|
| **Country of Origin** | England | India |
| **Trigger** | Escape of accumulated dangerous thing | Operation of hazardous enterprise |
| **Defences Available** | Yes (6 exceptions) | **None** (zero defences) |
| **Scope** | Private individuals, small scale, any accumulation | Industrial / commercial / enterprise scale |
| **Standard of Care** | Care not relevant to liability | Care not a defence at all |
| **Measure of Damages** | Reasonable compensation | Proportionate to harm + capacity of enterprise |
| **Status in India** | Adopted, but partially overridden | Supreme law for hazardous industries |
| **Insurance Aspect** | Not inherently required | Implicitly expected (PLI Act, 1991 enacted) |
| **Offence Type** | Tortious (Civil) | Constitutional Tort (Article 21 — Right to Life) |
| **Modern Applicability** | Outdated for mass disasters | Designed for Bhopal-grade disasters |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **For Civil Engineers**: Strict liability principles apply to design, construction, and maintenance of dams, reservoirs, chemical storage tanks, and structures retaining water or toxic substances.
- **For Chemical Engineers**: Direct application to all plants handling substances above PLI Act threshold quantities — affects plant design, insurance premiums, and risk assessment.
- **For Mechanical Engineers**: Operation of boilers, pressure vessels, and heavy machinery — falls under hazardous activity doctrine.
- **For Environmental Engineers**: Critical in conducting EIA (Environmental Impact Assessment) studies and disaster preparedness planning.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Case Law Analysis & Legal Matrix

## 3.1 Statutory Relief Calculation Framework under PLI Act

### Formula 1: Relief for Fatal Accident
$$
R_{fatal} = R_{max}
$$
where $R_{fatal}$ is the relief amount for death, fixed at the maximum statutory limit notified by the Government.

### Formula 2: Relief for Permanent Total Disability
$$
R_{PTD} = R_{fatal}
$$
Permanent Total Disability (e.g., loss of both eyes, both limbs) is treated at par with death.

### Formula 3: Relief for Permanent Partial Disability
$$
R_{PPD} = R_{fatal} \times \frac{\% \, disability \, certified}{100}
$$
The percentage of disability is certified by a medical board.

### Formula 4: Relief for Temporary Total Disability (Hospitalization)
$$
R_{TTD} = R_{daily} \times N_{days}
$$
where $R_{daily}$ is the daily relief rate and $N_{days}$ is the number of days of hospitalization.

### Formula 5: Aggregate Liability Cap
$$
L_{max} = \min(C_{paid-up}, \, L_{notified})
$$
The owner's total liability is the **lesser of**: (a) the **paid-up capital** of the undertaking, OR (b) the **maximum amount notified** by the Central Government.

---

## 3.2 Detailed Case Law Analysis: *Rylands v Fletcher* (1868)

### Facts of the Case
The defendant, **John Rylands**, employed independent contractors to build a **reservoir** on his land to supply water to his mill. The contractors negligently failed to block up some old mine shafts under the site. When the reservoir was filled, water burst through the disused mine shafts and flooded the plaintiff's (**Thomas Fletcher's**) mine on adjoining land.

### Procedural History
- **Trial Court**: Held in favour of the plaintiff (Fletcher).
- **Court of Exchequer (1866)**: Reversed the trial court's decision — held that there was **no evidence of negligence** by Rylands.
- **Court of Exchequer Chamber (1868)**: Reversed the Court of Exchequer — held Rylands **strictly liable**.
- **House of Lords (1868)**: Upheld the Court of Exchequer Chamber — **Rylands liable** even without negligence.

### Reasoning (Per Blackburn J.)
- The defendant who brings onto his land, and keeps there, a thing likely to cause mischief if it escapes, is **prima facie** answerable for all the damage which is the natural consequence of its escape.
- The justification is that a person who chooses to engage in a **non-natural use of land**, accumulating a dangerous substance, is essentially creating a **risk to his neighbours**.
- Liability is imposed because the defendant has the **opportunity to make internal arrangements** (insurance, precautions) to cover this risk — not the plaintiff.

### Legal Rule Established
$$
\text{Strict Liability} = f(\text{Non-natural use}, \text{Dangerous thing}, \text{Escape}, \text{Damage})
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Memory Aid:** "**Bringing, Keeping, Non-Natural, Escape, Mischief**" — the **BKNEM** test for strict liability.

---

## 3.3 Detailed Case Law Analysis: *MC Mehta v Union of India* (Oleum Gas Leak Case, 1987)

### Facts
- The **Shriram Food and Fertiliser Industries** operated a fertilizer plant containing large quantities of **oleum (concentrated sulphuric acid)** in **Delhi**.
- On **4th & 6th December 1985**, oleum gas leaked from the plant, affecting nearby residents (one person died, several fell sick).
- A Public Interest Litigation (PIL) was filed by **MC Mehta**, an environmentalist lawyer.

### Issues Before the Supreme Court
1. Should the rule in *Rylands v Fletcher* be adopted in India for hazardous industries?
2. Should exceptions to strict liability be made available to such industries?
3. What is the appropriate measure of damages?

### Judgment (Per Bhagwati J.)
- The Supreme Court **adopted** the rule of strict liability from *Rylands v Fletcher* for the purpose of the Constitution of India.
- However, the Court found the rule **inadequate for modern hazardous industries** — its exceptions were "exploited" by large industrialists.
- The Court evolved the **new doctrine of Absolute Liability** with the following features:
  1. The rule of absolute liability is a **rule of strict liability sans exceptions**.
  2. The enterprise is **absolutely and unconditionally liable** to compensate for harm caused.
  3. The measure of damages must be **co-extensive with the magnitude and capacity of the enterprise**.
  4. The principle is grounded in **Article 21 (Right to Life and Livelihood)** of the Indian Constitution.

### The "Hazardous or Inherently Dangerous" Test
The Court laid down that an activity is "hazardous or inherently dangerous" if:
- It involves a **risk of harm to persons living in the surrounding area**, OR
- The **magnitude of the harm**, if it occurs, is **grave**, OR
- The enterprise is engaged in manufacturing, processing, treating, or storing any hazardous substance.

### Subsequent Application: Bhopal Gas Tragedy
In **Union Carbide Corporation v Union of India (1989)**, the Supreme Court applied the absolute liability principle and accepted the **$470 million settlement** from Union Carbide as "just, fair and reasonable" compensation for Bhopal victims — a landmark application of the doctrine.

---

## 3.4 The PLI Act vs. Absolute Liability — A Working Analytical Matrix

| Statutory Question | PLI Act, 1991 | Absolute Liability Doctrine |
|---|---|---|
| **Type of Law** | Statutory (legislative enactment) | Judge-made (judicial doctrine) |
| **Who is Liable?** | Owner of hazardous substance | The enterprise |
| **Standard of Fault** | No-fault (statutory) | No-fault (absolute) |
| **Quantum of Relief** | Statutory caps (e.g., Rs. 5 lakh) | Open-ended, based on harm + capacity |
| **Forum of Claim** | District Collector (administrative) | Civil Court / Supreme Court (judicial) |
| **Defences** | Limited | **None** |
| **Pioneering Case** | Bhopal Gas Tragedy (1984) | Oleum Gas Leak (1985) |
| **Scope** | Threshold-quantity hazardous substances | All hazardous enterprises |

### How PLI Act, 1991 Was Born — The Sequence
1. **Bhopal Gas Tragedy (December 1984)** — World's worst industrial disaster.
2. **Oleum Gas Leak (December 1985)** — India's own domestic disaster.
3. **MC Mehta Judgment (1987)** — Absolute Liability doctrine evolved.
4. **PLI Act, 1991** — Legislative follow-up to ensure **statutory insurance** against absolute liability.
5. **National Environment Tribunal Act, 1995** — Appellate mechanism established.
6. **Public Liability Insurance Rules, 1991** — Procedural and threshold details.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Error:** Students often confuse "Strict Liability" with "Absolute Liability." Remember the **One-Word Distinction**: **Strict = Defences exist. Absolute = Zero defences.** The moment you write "no defence is available" for a strict liability question, you have switched to absolute liability and lost marks.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Operational Framework of PLI Act, 1991

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Hazardous Industry Owner] -->|Identifies Threshold Substance| B[Public Liability Insurance Act, 1991 Triggered]
    B --> C[Mandatory Insurance Policy Section 4]
    C --> D[Industrial Accident Occurs]
    D --> E[Victim Files Claim with District Collector]
    E --> F{Is Claim Valid?}
    F -->|Yes| G[Relief Awarded as per Schedule]
    F -->|No| H[Claim Rejected with Reasons]
    G --> I[Payment from Insurance Policy]
    G --> J{Insurance Inadequate?}
    J -->|Yes| K[Environmental Relief Fund Section 7A]
    J -->|No| L[Case Closed]
    H --> M[Appeal to Claims Commissioner]
    G --> N[Appeal to National Environment Tribunal]
    N --> O[Final Appeal to Supreme Court]
    K --> L
    M --> P[Collector Review]
    P -->|Allowed| G
    P -->|Rejected| N
```

## 4.2 Schematic: Application Test of Strict Liability

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Defendant Brings Dangerous Thing on Land] --> B{Is it Non-Natural Use?}
    B -->|No| C[Rule Does Not Apply]
    B -->|Yes| D{Likely to Cause Mischief?}
    D -->|No| C
    D -->|Yes| E{Escape Occurred?}
    E -->|No| C
    E -->|Yes| F{Damage Caused?}
    F -->|No| C
    F -->|Yes| G[Strict Liability Established]
    G --> H{Any of 6 Defences Available?}
    H -->|Yes Act of God| I[Defendant Escapes Liability]
    H -->|Yes Act of Third Party| I
    H -->|Yes Plaintiffs Default| I
    H -->|Yes Statutory Authority| I
    H -->|Yes Consent| I
    H -->|Yes Common Benefit| I
    H -->|No| J[Defendant Strictly Liable]
```

## 4.3 Schematic: Absolute Liability Test (No Escape Route)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Enterprise Operating Hazardous Activity] --> B{Activity Inherently Dangerous?}
    B -->|No| C[Normal Negligence Standard Applies]
    B -->|Yes| D{Harm to Person or Property?}
    D -->|No| C
    D -->|Yes| E{Causation Established?}
    E -->|No| C
    E -->|Yes| F[Absolute Liability Fixed]
    F --> G{Any Defence Claimed?}
    G -->|Yes Act of God| H[Defence Rejected]
    G -->|Yes Contributory Negligence| H
    G -->|Yes Care Taken Precautions| H
    G -->|Yes Third Party Act| H
    G -->|No Defence Claimed| I[Compensation Determined]
    H --> I
    I --> J{Compensation Quantum}
    J --> K[Proportionate to Magnitude of Harm]
    J --> L[Commensurate with Capacity of Enterprise]
    K --> M[Order of Court for Payment]
    L --> M
```

## 4.4 Comparative Block: Strict Liability vs Absolute Liability

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SL [Strict Liability Rylands v Fletcher 1868]
        SL1[Trigger Escape of Dangerous Thing] --> SL2[Liability Arises Automatically]
        SL2 --> SL3[Six Defences Available]
        SL3 --> SL4[Defence Pleaded]
        SL4 -->|Success| SL5[Defendant Escapes]
        SL4 -->|Failure| SL6[Defendant Liable]
    end
    
    subgraph AL [Absolute Liability MC Mehta v UOI 1987]
        AL1[Trigger Hazardous Enterprise Activity] --> AL2[Liability Arises Automatically]
        AL2 --> AL3[Zero Defences Allowed]
        AL3 --> AL4[No Plea of Care Possible]
        AL4 --> AL5[Enterprise Liable Unconditionally]
    end
```

## 4.5 Process Flow: Filing of Claim under PLI Act, 1991

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Accident with Hazardous Substance] --> B[Victim or Legal Heir]
    B --> C[File Application to District Collector]
    C --> D{Acknowledgment within 7 Days}
    D --> E[Medical Board Assesses Injury]
    E --> F[Collector Adjudicates Claim]
    F --> G{Valid Claim?}
    G -->|Yes| H[Relief Amount Calculated]
    G -->|No| I[Claim Rejected with Reasons]
    H --> J[Disbursement via Insurance Company]
    H --> K{Disbursement within 3 Months}
    K -->|No| L[Interest Payable]
    K -->|Yes| M[Case Closed]
    I --> N[Appeal to Claims Commissioner within 30 Days]
    N --> O{Appeal Allowed?}
    O -->|Yes| H
    O -->|No| P[Appeal to National Environment Tribunal]
    P --> Q{Allowed?}
    Q -->|Yes| H
    Q -->|No| R[Appeal to Supreme Court]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define the Public Liability Insurance Act, 1991 and state its primary objective. [3 Marks]
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]**

**Model Answer:**
The Public Liability Insurance Act, 1991 is an Indian environmental legislation enacted to provide for **mandatory public liability insurance** for immediate relief to persons affected by **accidents occurring while handling hazardous substances**. The Act's primary objective is to ensure **speedy, no-fault compensation** to victims of industrial accidents (death, injury, or property damage) arising from handling hazardous substances exceeding the threshold quantity specified in the Schedule, without requiring the victim to prove negligence or fault on the part of the owner. It was enacted as a direct legislative response to the **Bhopal Gas Tragedy (1984)**.

---

### Question 2: State the rule of Absolute Liability as laid down in *MC Mehta v Union of India*. [3 Marks]
**[KTU University Exam - December 2023, 2024 Scheme]**

**Model Answer:**
The rule of Absolute Liability was evolved by the Supreme Court of India in ***MC Mehta v Union of India* (Oleum Gas Leak Case, 1987)**. The rule states that an enterprise engaged in a **hazardous or inherently dangerous activity** is **absolutely and unconditionally liable** for any harm caused to persons, property, or the environment as a consequence of such activity. **No defence** is available to the enterprise, including the defences available under *Rylands v Fletcher*. The compensation must be **proportionate to the magnitude of harm** and **commensurate with the capacity of the enterprise** to pay — embodying the "polluter pays" principle. The doctrine is grounded in **Article 21** (Right to Life) of the Constitution.

---

## PART B: Long-Answer Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU ESE Pattern 2024:** Each 14-mark question must be answered with sub-parts. The model answers below provide exhaustive, 7-mark sub-answers to ensure full marks.

---

### Question A (14 Marks): Discuss in detail the salient features and operational mechanism of the Public Liability Insurance Act, 1991. How does the Act provide for compensation in case of death, injury, and property damage?

#### Part (a) [7 Marks]: Salient Features and Operational Mechanism of PLI Act

**[KTU University Exam - December 2023, 2024 Scheme]**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Background and Need:**
The PLI Act, 1991 was enacted in the wake of the **Bhopal Gas Tragedy (December 1984)** to ensure that victims of industrial accidents involving hazardous substances receive **immediate, no-fault relief** without prolonged litigation. It came into force on **1st April 1991**.

**2. Scope and Application (Section 2):**
The Act applies to every **"owner"** handling a **"hazardous substance"** in **quantity equal to or exceeding the threshold quantity** specified in **Schedule I** of the Public Liability Insurance Rules, 1991. The term "owner" includes the person in actual possession, control, or custody of the hazardous substance.

**3. Mandatory Insurance (Section 4):**
The owner is **mandatorily required** to take out an **Insurance Policy** before handling the hazardous substance. The policy must:
- Cover liability for **death, bodily injury, or property damage** to third parties.
- Be for an amount equivalent to the **paid-up capital** of the undertaking or the **maximum amount notified** by the Central Government, whichever is **less**.

**4. Establishment of Environmental Relief Fund (Section 7A):**
A fund was established to meet:
- Relief in cases where the owner fails to pay.
- Excess claims beyond the insurance cover.
- Interim relief pending final settlement.

**5. Administration — Role of District Collector:**
The **District Collector** of the affected district is the designated authority to:
- Adjudicate all claims for relief.
- Determine the relief amount as per the **Schedule** of the Act.
- Ensure disbursement within the prescribed time limit.

**6. Appellate Hierarchy:**
The Act provides a three-tier appeal mechanism:
- **Claims Commissioner** (first appeal, within 30 days).
- **National Environment Tribunal** (second appeal).
- **Supreme Court** (final appellate authority).

**7. Statutory Relief Limits:**
The Act prescribes specific relief amounts (subject to revision) for:
- Death: Up to **Rs. 5,00,000**.
- Permanent Total Disability: Up to **Rs. 5,00,000**.
- Permanent Partial Disability: Proportionate to disability %.
- Temporary Total Disability: Daily rate × days hospitalized.

---

#### Part (b) [7 Marks]: Compensation for Death, Injury, and Property Damage under the PLI Act

**Model Answer:**

**1. For Death (Fatal Accidents):**
The legal heir(s) of the deceased may apply to the District Collector within the **prescribed limitation period**. Upon verification, the Collector awards a **lump sum relief** up to the maximum statutory amount (currently **Rs. 5,00,000**). The payment is made **ex gratia** (as a matter of grace) and does not preclude the claimant from pursuing civil remedies for higher damages separately.

**2. For Bodily Injury:**
The Act provides differentiated relief:
- **Permanent Total Disability** (e.g., loss of both eyes): Equal to fatal accident relief.
- **Permanent Partial Disability** (e.g., loss of one limb): Calculated as a percentage of the fatal accident relief, based on the disability certified by a medical board.
- **Temporary Total Disability**: A daily rate for each day of hospitalization, plus a reasonable amount for treatment, medicines, and loss of livelihood.
- **Loss of Livelihood**: Reasonable monthly compensation during the disability period.

**3. For Property Damage:**
The Act covers damage to **private property** of third parties (houses, vehicles, crops, livestock) caused by the accident. The Collector determines compensation based on the **actual loss** assessed by revenue authorities and expert valuers. There is a **cap** based on the owner's insurance cover or paid-up capital.

**4. No-Fault Principle:**
The most significant feature is that the claimant need **not prove negligence, fault, or wrongful act**. The mere fact of an **accident** involving the hazardous substance and the **resulting damage** is sufficient to invoke liability. This is the **revolutionary shift** from traditional tort principles.

**5. Time Limit and Disbursement:**
The Collector must dispose of the claim within **3 months** of receipt. Delay beyond this attracts **interest** on the relief amount. The payment is made from the owner's insurance policy first; the Environmental Relief Fund is the secondary source.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): Explain the rule of Strict Liability as laid down in *Rylands v Fletcher*. What are its exceptions? How does it differ from the rule of Absolute Liability in India?

#### Part (a) [7 Marks]: The Rule in *Rylands v Fletcher* and Its Exceptions

**[KTU University Exam - May 2023, 2024 Scheme]**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Historical Background:**
The rule was laid down by **Justice Blackburn** in ***Rylands v Fletcher* (1868)**. The defendant, John Rylands, employed contractors to build a reservoir on his land for his mill. The contractors carelessly failed to block up some disused mine shafts under the site. When the reservoir was filled, water burst through the old shafts and flooded the plaintiff's (Thomas Fletcher's) adjoining mine. Although the defendant was **not personally negligent**, the courts held him liable.

**2. The Legal Rule:**
The rule states that **a person who, for his own purposes, brings onto his land and collects and keeps there anything likely to cause mischief if it escapes, is strictly liable for all damage which is the natural consequence of its escape**.

**3. Essential Conditions (BKNEM Test):**
- **B**ringing the substance onto the land.
- **K**eeping it accumulated for his own use.
- **N**on-natural use of land (extraordinary, unusual).
- **E**scape of the substance.
- **M**ischief/damage to the plaintiff.

**4. Six Exceptions (Defences Available to the Defendant):**
1. **Act of God (Vis Major):** An unforeseeable natural disaster (e.g., unprecedented flood, earthquake) that could not have been anticipated.
2. **Act of Third Party / Stranger:** An independent, unauthorized act of a third party not attributable to the defendant's negligence.
3. **Plaintiff's Own Default:** The plaintiff contributed to the damage through his own negligence or deliberate act.
4. **Statutory Authority:** The accumulation was lawfully required by a statute (e.g., municipal waterworks).
5. **Consent of the Plaintiff:** The plaintiff voluntarily agreed to bear the risk.
6. **Common Benefit:** The accumulation was for the mutual benefit of both plaintiff and defendant (e.g., a shared irrigation tank).

**5. Adoption in India:**
The rule was adopted in India in the case of ***Municipal Corporation of Delhi v Subhagwanti* (1966)**, where the Delhi High Court applied it to a building collapse.

---

#### Part (b) [7 Marks]: Differences between Strict Liability and Absolute Liability

**Model Answer:**

**1. Origin:**
- **Strict Liability**: Originated in England in 1868 (*Rylands v Fletcher*).
- **Absolute Liability**: Evolved in India in 1987 (*MC Mehta v UoI*) by Justice P.N. Bhagwati.

**2. Scope:**
- **Strict Liability**: Applies to any person who brings and accumulates a dangerous thing on land — covers individuals, small-scale operations, and large industries alike.
- **Absolute Liability**: Applies specifically to **enterprises** engaged in hazardous or inherently dangerous activities — a more focused, industry-specific doctrine.

**3. Defences:**
- **Strict Liability**: Six defences are available (Act of God, Act of Third Party, Plaintiff's Default, Statutory Authority, Consent, Common Benefit).
- **Absolute Liability**: **No defence** is available — not even the defences under *Rylands v Fletcher*.

**4. Standard of Care:**
- **Strict Liability**: The defendant is liable regardless of care, but care may be relevant to defences (e.g., if precautions were sufficient, the defendant may claim "no negligence attributable").
- **Absolute Liability**: The defendant is liable **even if all possible care and precautions were taken**. The magnitude of precaution is irrelevant to liability.

**5. Measure of Damages:**
- **Strict Liability**: Reasonable compensation for the damage suffered.
- **Absolute Liability**: Compensation **co-extensive with the magnitude of the harm** AND **proportionate to the capacity of the enterprise** to pay (Polluter Pays Principle).

**6. Constitutional Foundation:**
- **Strict Liability**: A pure common law tort principle.
- **Absolute Liability**: Grounded in **Article 21 (Right to Life)** of the Indian Constitution — environmental protection as a fundamental right.

**7. Modern Applicability:**
- **Strict Liability**: Found somewhat outdated for modern industrial hazards.
- **Absolute Liability**: Designed for **post-Bhopal** Indian industrial landscape — a stronger tool for victim compensation.

**8. Justification:**
- **Strict Liability**: Based on the principle of **"sic utere tuo ut alienum non laedas"** (use your own property so as not to injure another).
- **Absolute Liability**: Based on the principle that an enterprise **profiting from a hazardous activity** must bear the full cost of any harm — a **social cost internalization** principle.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Students Marks (Compiled from KTU 2019–2023 Valuation Patterns):**
> 1. **Conflating Strict with Absolute Liability** — Writing "no defence is available" under *Rylands v Fletcher* is a **fatal error**. Memorize the **Six Exceptions** cold.
> 2. **Forgetting the "Non-Natural Use" trigger** — Strict liability requires **non-natural use of land**, not merely any dangerous thing. Many students skip this condition.
> 3. **Missing the "Escape" requirement** — If the dangerous thing has **not escaped** from the defendant's control, strict liability does not arise (e.g., workers injured inside the plant).
> 4. **Omitting case citations** — In the PLI Act, mention **Bhopal Gas Tragedy, 1984**. In *MC Mehta*, mention **Oleum Gas Leak, 1985** and **Article 21**. In *Rylands*, mention **Justice Blackburn**.
> 5. **Not mentioning the "Polluter Pays" Principle** — This is a **mandatory phrase** in any answer on absolute liability.
> 6. **Wrong Statutory Amounts** — Quote relief amounts with **Rs. 5,00,000** or its revised notified value, not fabricated numbers.
> 7. **Ignoring the threshold quantity clause** in PLI Act — The Act applies **only above** the threshold quantity; do not write that it applies to all hazardous substances.
> 8. **Skipping Article 21 linkage** in *MC Mehta* — Linking absolute liability to the **Right to Life** is a **scoring point** worth 1-2 marks.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### Public Liability Insurance Act, 1991 — Quick Recall
- **Full Form**: Public Liability Insurance Act, 1991.
- **Date of Enforcement**: **1st April 1991**.
- **Triggered By**: Bhopal Gas Tragedy (1984).
- **Applies To**: Owners handling hazardous substances above the **threshold quantity** in Schedule I.
- **Key Provision**: **Mandatory public liability insurance** under Section 4.
- **Claim Forum**: **District Collector** of the affected district.
- **Relief Cap**: Up to **Rs. 5,00,000** for death / permanent total disability.
- **Time Limit for Disbursement**: **3 months** from claim filing.
- **Appellate Hierarchy**: Claims Commissioner → National Environment Tribunal → Supreme Court.
- **Environmental Relief Fund**: Section 7A — back-up fund for inadequate insurance.
- **Number of Sections**: **26 Sections** + a Schedule.

### Strict Liability — Quick Recall
- **Case**: ***Rylands v Fletcher* (1868)**.
- **Court**: House of Lords (UK).
- **Author**: **Justice Blackburn**.
- **Trigger**: **BKNEM Test** (Bringing, Keeping, Non-Natural use, Escape, Mischief).
- **Defences (6)**: Act of God, Act of Third Party, Plaintiff's Default, Statutory Authority, Consent, Common Benefit.
- **Indian Adoption**: ***Municipal Corporation of Delhi v Subhagwanti* (1966)**.
- **Limitation**: Applies to **escape** outside the defendant's control — injuries inside the plant not covered (*Read v Lyons*).

### Absolute Liability — Quick Recall
- **Case**: ***MC Mehta v Union of India* (1987)** — Oleum Gas Leak Case.
- **Court**: Supreme Court of India.
- **Author**: **Justice P.N. Bhagwati**.
- **Trigger**: Hazardous or inherently dangerous activity by an enterprise.
- **Defences**: **NIL** (Zero defences).
- **Constitutional Basis**: **Article 21** — Right to Life.
- **Key Principle**: **Polluter Pays** — compensation = harm × capacity to pay.
- **Application in Bhopal**: ***Union Carbide Corporation v UoI* (1989)** — $470 million settlement.
- **One-Line Distinction**: "Strict liability is liability without fault with limited defences; absolute liability is liability without fault with **no defences**."

### KTU 2024 Exam Strategy — Final 3 Points
1. **Memorize 6 Exceptions** to *Rylands v Fletcher* — direct 3-mark question almost every semester.
2. **Quote the Case** in any 14-mark question — *Rylands v Fletcher* (1868) for strict liability; *MC Mehta v UoI* (1987) for absolute liability.
3. **Connect to Bhopal Gas Tragedy** in any PLI Act answer — it is the historical and legislative origin of the statute.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: International Environmental Law: An Introduction to International Law
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET418 - ENVIRONMENTAL LAWS AND POLICY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** International Environmental Law: An Introduction to International Law

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is International Law?

**International Law** is the body of legal rules, norms, and principles that govern the relations and interactions between sovereign States and other entities recognized as subjects of international personality. Unlike domestic law, it lacks a single centralized legislature, executive, or compulsory judiciary — yet it functions as a binding legal system through consent, custom, and treaty obligations.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Box**
> Per **Article 38(1) of the Statute of the International Court of Justice (ICJ)**, the sources of International Law are explicitly enumerated as: (a) International Conventions (Treaties), (b) International Custom, (c) General Principles of Law, and (d) Judicial Decisions and Scholarly Writings (as subsidiary means).

### Conceptual Analogy: The "Global Apartment Complex"

Imagine a giant apartment complex with **195 independent families** (Sovereign States) living in separate flats. Each family has its own internal rules for their flat. But they share common corridors, the water tank, the parking lot, and the garden. **International Law** is the *set of common rules* these families agree upon so that:
- No one dumps garbage into the shared water tank (No-Harm Principle).
- Everyone pays for the water they use (Polluter Pays / Common but Differentiated Responsibility).
- Loud music late at night disturbs neighbors (Transboundary Pollution).
- The building committee (UN) can warn rule-breakers, but has no police to drag them out (No central enforcement).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does this matter for an Engineer?**
> As future environmental engineers, you may design projects (dams, factories, power plants) that have transboundary impacts — a river flowing into Bangladesh, air pollution crossing into Sri Lanka, or e-waste shipped to Africa.
