# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define the Common but Differentiated Responsibilities (CBDR) principle. Mention its legal source. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Model Answer:**
CBDR is the principle of international environmental law which recognizes that while all states have a **common responsibility** to protect the global environment, they bear **differentiated responsibilities** based on their historical contribution to environmental degradation and their respective economic and technological capabilities. Its **legal source** is **Principle 7 of the 1992 Rio Declaration on Environment and Development**, and it has been operationalized through the **UNFCCC (1992)**, the **Kyoto Protocol (1997)**, and the **Paris Agreement (2015)**.

### Question 2: What is the Polluter Pays Principle? State its significance. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Model Answer:**
The Polluter Pays Principle (PPP) is an environmental policy principle (Rio Principle 16, 1992) that requires the entity responsible for causing pollution to bear the cost of managing, preventing, and remedying the environmental damage. **Significance:** (i) It internalizes environmental externalities, (ii) acts as a deterrent against pollution, (iii) ensures environmental justice for victims, and (iv) promotes the adoption of cleaner technologies by industries.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain in detail the origin, evolution, and legal status of the Common but Differentiated Responsibilities (CBDR) principle in international environmental law. Discuss its role in the UNFCCC framework with special reference to the Paris Agreement. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Critically evaluate the Polluter Pays Principle (PPP) as an instrument of environmental governance. Discuss its application in Indian environmental jurisprudence with relevant case laws. **(7 Marks)**

### Question A — Model Solution

#### (a) CBDR: Origin, Evolution, and Role in UNFCCC
- **Origin:** Emerged in the 1972 Stockholm Conference on the Human Environment; the concept of "differential treatment" of developing countries was formally articulated in the **1972 Declaration of the UN Conference on the Human Environment (Principle 12)**.
- **Evolution:** Consolidated in **Principle 7 of the 1992 Rio Declaration**. Operationalized through the **UNFCCC (1992)** which classified Parties into Annex I (developed) and Non-Annex I (developing) countries.
- **Kyoto Protocol (1997):** Imposed binding emission reduction targets only on **Annex I Parties** (e.g., EU committed to 8% reduction; USA signed but did not ratify).
- **Paris Agreement (2015):** Introduced **Nationally Determined Contributions (NDCs)** — a hybrid model where all countries submit targets, but the principle of CBDR is preserved through the principle of **"common but differentiated responsibilities and respective capabilities, in the light of different national circumstances"** (Article 4, paragraph 3).
- **Significance:** CBDR is the bedrock of climate justice and ensures that global environmental action does not entrench global economic inequality.

#### (b) Polluter Pays Principle — Critical Evaluation
- **Conceptual Strengths:**
  - Promotes the **internalization of externalities** in cost accounting.
  - Provides a **fiscal tool** (Pigouvian taxes) for governments to fund environmental remediation.
  - Creates a **deterrent effect** on polluters.
- **Limitations:**
  - Does not address **cumulative or historical pollution** adequately.
  - May be reduced to mere **licensing fees** if not enforced strictly.
  - Ignores **pollution by the poor** (e.g., subsistence-driven deforestation) — equity dimension.
- **Application in Indian Jurisprudence:**
  - **Indian Council for Enviro-Legal Action v. Union of India (1996):** The Supreme Court invoked PPP to hold chemical industries in Rajasthan liable for soil and groundwater contamination in Bichhri village.
  - **Vellore Citizens Welfare Forum v. Union of India (1996):** Court applied the **Precautionary Principle** and **PPP** together as part of the law of the land under Article 21.
  - **Sterlite Industries Case (2013):** The Madras High Court and Supreme Court references upheld PPP while directing compensation from the polluting copper smelter.
  - **National Green Tribunal Act, 2010:** Codifies PPP by empowering the NGT to order **environmental compensation** from polluters.

---

### Question B: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a)** Discuss the three pillars of the Common but Differentiated Responsibilities (CBDR) principle. How is it operationalized in the UNFCCC framework through the Annex classification? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the Polluter Pays Principle (PPP). Discuss its relationship with the Precautionary Principle. Illustrate with examples from the Indian environmental regulatory framework. **(7 Marks)**

### Question B — Model Solution

#### (a) Three Pillars of CBDR
1. **Common Responsibility:** All states share the obligation to protect the global environment — Earth is a single ecological system with shared atmospheric and oceanic commons.
2. **Differentiated Responsibility (Historical):** The cumulative GHG concentrations are a result of post-Industrial Revolution emissions primarily from developed countries; therefore, those nations bear greater responsibility.
3. **Differentiated Responsibility (Capability):** Nations differ in their economic and technological capacity; richer nations must support poorer ones through finance and technology transfer (e.g., Green Climate Fund).

**Operationalization via UNFCCC Annex Classification:**
- **Annex I Parties:** Industrialized OECD members + Economies in Transition (EITs). Bear **mandatory quantified emission limitation and reduction objectives (QELROs)**.
- **Annex II Parties:** Subset of Annex I (excluding EITs). Bear additional obligations to provide **financial and technological assistance** to developing countries.
- **Non-Annex I Parties:** Developing countries; no binding emission targets but must report on mitigation and adaptation actions.

#### (b) PPP and its Relationship with Precautionary Principle
- **PPP Definition:** Polluter must bear the cost of pollution control, remediation, and compensation (Rio Principle 16, 1992).
- **Precautionary Principle Definition:** Where there are threats of serious or irreversible damage, lack of full scientific certainty shall **not** be used as a reason for postponing cost-effective measures to prevent environmental degradation (Rio Principle 15, 1992).
- **Relationship:** Both principles are **complementary, not mutually exclusive**.
  - PPP is **ex-post** (after pollution occurs) — assigns liability.
  - Precautionary Principle is **ex-ante** (before damage occurs) — mandates preventive action.
  - Together, they form the **comprehensive environmental governance framework**.
- **Indian Framework Examples:**
  - **Water (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1974:** Empowers State Pollution Control Boards to levy **consent fees and environmental compensation**.
  - **Hazardous Waste Management Rules, 2016:** Mandates the **polluter** to manage, treat, store, and dispose of hazardous waste.
  - **E-Waste Management Rules, 2022:** Implements **Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR)** — a direct application of PPP.
  - **Public Liability Insurance Act, 1991:** Requires industries handling hazardous substances to **insure against** liability for accidents.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common Mistake #1:** Students often confuse **CBDR with Precautionary Principle**. CBDR is about *who pays/acts*; Precautionary Principle is about *when to act* (even without full scientific certainty).
> 2. **Common Mistake #2:** Writing that PPP is a "license to pollute" by paying money. **DO NOT** — PPP is a deterrent, not a permit. Examiners deduct up to 2 marks for this error.
> 3. **Common Mistake #3:** Citing case laws with wrong party names (e.g., confusing *Vellore Citizens* with *Vellore Residents*). Always verify: **Vellore Citizens Welfare Forum v. Union of India (1996) — 5 Judge Bench, AIR 1996 SC 2715**.
> 4. **Common Mistake #4:** Treating CBDR as a principle applicable **only** to climate change. CBDR is a **general principle** of international environmental law — it applies to ozone depletion (Montreal Protocol, 1987), biodiversity (Convention on Biological Diversity, 1992), and even fisheries management.
> 5. **Common Mistake #5:** Failing to mention the **Rio Declaration (1992)** as the primary source. While UNFCCC operationalizes CBDR, the *declarative* legal source is Rio Principle 7. Always cite both.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- **CBDR** = **Principle 7, Rio Declaration, 1992.** Codified in UNFCCC, Kyoto Protocol, Paris Agreement.
- **CBDR has 3 pillars:** Common Responsibility, Historical Differentiation, Capability-Based Differentiation.
- **Annex I = Developed, Annex II = Developed + Donor, Non-Annex I = Developing, LDCs = Special needs.**
- **PPP** = **Principle 16, Rio Declaration, 1992.** First adopted by **OECD in 1972**.
- **PPP is a DETERRENT, not a LICENSE to pollute.**
- **PPP is ex-post (after the fact); Precautionary Principle is ex-ante (before the fact).**
- **PPP works through:** Pigouvian Tax, Environmental Compensation Orders, Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR), and Public Liability Insurance.
- **Key Indian Cases:**
  - *Vellore Citizens Welfare Forum v. UoI (1996)* — Established PPP and Precautionary as part of Article 21.
  - *Indian Council for Enviro-Legal Action v. UoI (1996)* — Chemical industry liability in Bichhri.
  - *Sterlite Industries v. TNPCB (2013)* — PPP and compensation.
- **NGT Act, 2010** = Statutory body empowered to apply PPP and order compensation.
- **Loss and Damage Fund** = Established at **COP27 (Sharm el-Sheikh, 2022)** — operationalizes CBDR in the climate context.
- **Numerical anchor:** Current atmospheric CO₂ ≈ **424 ppm** (2024); Pre-industrial level = **280 ppm**; 1.5°C threshold = **~350 ppm equivalent**.
- **India's stand on CBDR:** Recognizes CBDR but insists on **"dynamic" differentiation** reflecting current realities (per-capita emissions now exceed the global average for several Annex I countries).
- **Mnemonic for CBDR pillars: "C-H-C"** — **C**ommon, **H**istorical, **C**apability.
- **Mnemonic for PPP instruments: "T-C-E-I"** — **T**ax (Pigouvian), **C**ompensation, **E**PR, **I**nsurance.
